name: Korea's Film Schools & Netflix: Timeline of Systemic Sexual Violence and Institutional Betrayal (2009–2026)
description: A decade-plus of sexual violence, institutional betrayal, and industry accountability failures across Korea's film schools and K-pop ecosystem, with particular focus on Netflix's documentary knowledge problem and content investment in documented exploitation systems.

item.name: Actress Jang Ja-yeon Dies by Suicide; NHRC Investigation Reveals 60.2% of Actresses Pressured for Sexual Services
item.description: Actress Jang Ja-yeon dies by suicide, leaving behind a letter alleging she was forced to provide sexual services to industry figures. A subsequent National Human Rights Commission investigation finds 60.2% of South Korean actresses had been asked to provide sexual services to industry stakeholders.

item.name: Nth Room Case: Telegram Mass Sexual Exploitation Network Operates 2018–2020
item.description: Cho Ju-bin — a graduate of Inha Technical College — operates a Telegram-based mass sexual exploitation network targeting young women and girls across South Korea. At minimum 74 women are directly blackmailed. Approximately 260,000 users participate across linked chat rooms. Cho is sentenced to 47 years in prison.

item.name: KWDI Study: 61.5% of Female University Arts Students Experience Sexual Violence
item.description: The Korean Women's Development Institute (KWDI) publishes its nationally representative study finding 61.5% of female students in Korean university arts and culture programs experience sexual violence, predominantly perpetrated by male-dominated faculty. Film departments score 81/100 on risk metrics.

item.name: KPop Demon Hunters Enters Production at Sony Pictures Animation
item.description: KPop Demon Hunters enters production at Sony Pictures Animation with the full creative team attached. Co-directed by Korean Canadian filmmaker Maggie Kang and Chris Appelhans.

item.name: Netflix Acquires KPop Demon Hunters IP, Distribution, and Music Rights from Sony
item.description: Sony Pictures Animation enters into a deal with Netflix for KPop Demon Hunters. Netflix acquires all IP, distribution, merchandise, and music rights. Sony retains production rights and a capped profit premium. Netflix becomes sole licensor of the franchise in perpetuity.

item.name: Netflix Produces Cyber Hell: Exposing an Internet Horror
item.description: Netflix produces and globally releases Cyber Hell: Exposing an Internet Horror, documenting the Telegram mass sexual exploitation network. Amnesty International describes the crimes as reflecting structural gender-based violence in South Korea.

item.name: Netflix Produces In the Name of God: A Holy Betrayal; Wins Korean Court Injunction
item.description: Netflix produces and globally distributes In the Name of God: A Holy Betrayal, documenting four Korean cult leaders who sexually exploited female followers recruited predominantly from universities. JMS files an injunction to suppress it. Netflix's legal team defeats the injunction in Seoul Western District Court.

item.name: Netflix Pledges $2.5 Billion USD to Korean Content Investment (2023–2027)
item.description: Netflix pledges $2.5 billion USD to Korean content investment covering 2023–2027, directly funding the ecosystem whose systemic exploitation conditions Netflix's own documentary productions document.

item.name: JYP Entertainment Announces TWICE 14th Mini Album Strategy
item.description: JYP Entertainment announces TWICE's 14th mini album Strategy, to be released December 6, 2024. The title track Strategy will later be licensed by Netflix for KPop Demon Hunters.

item.name: December 6, 2024: TWICE Releases Strategy AND KG Crown Files Lawsuit Against JYP USA
item.description: On the same day: TWICE releases 14th mini album Strategy via JYP Entertainment/Republic Records. Simultaneously, Kiera Grace Madder (KG Crown), 17, files KG Crown v. JYP USA Entertainment in Los Angeles County Superior Court. Allegations include hidden cameras, starvation-level dietary control, debt bondage ($500K), forced training through injury, and cover-up of a suicide attempt. She signed her contract with JYP at age 15.

item.name: California Court Denies JYP Motion to Compel Arbitration
item.description: California court denies JYP USA's motion to compel arbitration, ruling that alleged fraud in contract formation can nullify an arbitration clause — exposing the full record to public judicial review.

item.name: KG Crown Releases 132-Page Court Declaration Documenting JYP Abuse
item.description: KG Crown publicly releases her 132-page court declaration documenting the full scope of allegations against JYP USA: hidden cameras in the dining area, starvation-level dietary control, $500K debt bondage, forced training through injury, and cover-up of a suicide attempt (one member ingested 42 NyQuil pills; JYP staff instructed the girls to tell a child welfare worker it was food poisoning).

item.name: Gender Watchdog First Outreach to Netflix and Netflix Korea
item.description: The Gender Watchdog Research Collective sends urgent notifications to Netflix and Netflix Korea, warning of Title IX and reputational risks due to systemic sexual violence across Korean arts universities and their industry partners, including Netflix's $2.5 billion Korean content pipeline.

item.name: IEQAS Certification Crisis Exposé: Investigation Expands to Multiple Korean Universities
item.description: After government and university inaction for 34+ days, Gender Watchdog's investigation expands to systemic sexual violence issues across Korean higher education institutions. Findings reveal compromised IEQAS certification, with institutions falsifying partnerships for funding and maintaining programs with documented sexual violence risks.

item.name: KPop Demon Hunters Premieres Globally on Netflix with JYP/TWICE Soundtrack
item.description: KPop Demon Hunters premieres globally on Netflix. The soundtrack includes two TWICE/JYP-licensed tracks: Takedown (performed by Jeongyeon, Jihyo, Chaeyoung) and Strategy (solo version). Netflix's official page credits both as TWICE appears courtesy of JYP Entertainment Corporation. The JYP child exploitation lawsuit is in active litigation on this date.

item.name: VCHA Member Kaylee Departs via Mutual Contract Termination
item.description: VCHA member Kaylee departs JYP Entertainment via mutual contract termination. She was 15 at the time of original signing.

item.name: KG Crown v. JYP USA Entertainment Settles Under Sealed Terms
item.description: KG Crown v. JYP USA Entertainment settles under sealed terms, confirming KG's permanent departure from JYP Entertainment. Terms are not disclosed. A sealed settlement preserves the company's right not to confirm the facts. It does not constitute their denial.

item.name: Netflix and Sony Begin KPop Demon Hunters Sequel Negotiations; Echoes of Survivors Produced
item.description: Netflix and Sony Pictures Animation begin sequel negotiations for KPop Demon Hunters; directors Kang and Appelhans sign exclusive multi-year Netflix deals — in the same month the JYP lawsuit settles under seal. Simultaneously, Netflix produces The Echoes of Survivors: Inside Korea's Tragedies, continuing the JMS cult investigation and documenting how cover-up extends to police and government officials. JMS files a second injunction; Netflix defeats it again.

item.name: KPop Demon Hunters Deluxe Soundtrack Released
item.description: Deluxe version of the KPop Demon Hunters soundtrack released, including additional tracks and alternate versions.

item.name: Korea Times: "8 Out of 10 Female K-Pop Trainees Stop Menstruating"
item.description: Korea Times publishes coverage of journalist Jeon Da-hyeon's book K-pop, Idols in Wonderland, reporting the finding from 40+ industry insiders that eight out of 10 female trainees stop menstruating due to extreme dietary restriction under intense physical training — physiological evidence of the exploitation structure that produced TWICE, Itzy, Stray Kids, and every other JYP act currently on streaming platforms globally.

item.name: KPop Demon Hunters Brazilian Portuguese Soundtrack Released
item.description: Brazilian Portuguese edition of the KPop Demon Hunters soundtrack released globally.

item.name: South Korea Revised Standard Trainee Contracts Take Effect
item.description: South Korea's Ministry of Culture, Sports and Tourism's revised standard trainee contracts take effect — for the first time explicitly prohibiting verbal abuse, coercion, and sexual violence, adding these to prior contracts' existing prohibition on physical assault. The necessity of this legislation documents what the law said before 2026: nothing enforceable.

item.name: Scott Shepherd: "How Much Does K-Pop Cost?" — Korea Times Op-Ed
item.description: Scott Shepherd, British academic at a Korean university, publishes op-ed in Korea Times asking: "How many ruined lives is one successful K-pop star worth? What is the value of a million clicks if measured per objectified young man or woman, per sexualised child? Per life lost?" — naming the cycle of scandal-and-forgetting as structural.

item.name: Gender Watchdog Publishes Chung-Ang University Documentation
item.description: Gender Watchdog publishes documentation of Chung-Ang University: 27% reciprocity rate in claimed global partnerships; 21-year sexual violence cover-up record; Ministry-funded OTT content specialization pipeline into CJ ENM and Netflix. No rebuttals received.

item.name: Gender Watchdog Publishes "Nine Korean Universities, Zero Rebuttals"
item.description: Gender Watchdog publishes Nine Korean Universities, Zero Rebuttals — documenting that no Korean university has contested any finding across the global campaign notifying the world's top 1,500 universities of systematic partnership falsification.

item.name: KPop Demon Hunters Wins Best Animated Feature and Best Original Song at 98th Academy Awards
item.description: KPop Demon Hunters wins two Academy Awards at the 98th Oscars: Best Animated Feature and Best Original Song ("Golden"). The film has surpassed 500 million cumulative views on Netflix. The awards ceremony takes place on March 15, 2026; the Korea Times article covering it is dated March 16, 2026 (Korea is a day ahead of Los Angeles). Gender Watchdog's ESG notice to Netflix goes out on March 17, 2026.

item.name: Gender Watchdog Sends ESG Notice to Netflix; Publishes "Netflix Knew"
item.description: Gender Watchdog sends formal ESG notice to Netflix, Coupang Play, Motion Pictures Association, Disney, HBO/Max, and BHRRC documenting supply chain risk, university partnership fraud, and the Netflix documentary library knowledge problem. Simultaneously publishes Netflix Knew: The Documentary Library That Documents What Platform Investment Obscures.
